sections.title: Look like / Be like / Like
sections.instructions: Choose the correct question or answer. Remember: 'What does he/she look like?' = appearance, 'What is he/she like?' = personality, 'What does he/she like?' = preferences.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: He's tall and he's got short brown hair.
questions.explanation: We ask 'What does he look like?' when we want to know about someone's physical appearance.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: She's very friendly and kind.
questions.explanation: We ask 'What is she like?' when we want to know about someone's personality.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: He likes playing football and reading comics.
questions.explanation: We ask 'What does he like?' when we want to know about someone's hobbies or preferences.

questions.sentence: A: What does your mum look like? B: ___
options: She's got long brown hair and blue eyes.
questions.answer: She's got long brown hair and blue eyes.
questions.explanation: 'What does she look like?' asks about physical appearance — hair, eyes, height, etc.

questions.sentence: A: What is your best friend like? B: ___
questions.explanation: 'What is he like?' asks about personality — friendly, clever, kind, etc.

questions.sentence: A: What does your brother like? B: ___
questions.explanation: 'What does he like?' asks about hobbies and interests.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: She's got short grey hair and she's quite short.
options: What does your grandma look like?
questions.answer: What does your grandma look like?
questions.explanation: The answer describes physical appearance, so the question is 'What does she look like?'

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: He's very popular and brave.
questions.explanation: 'Popular' and 'brave' are personality traits, so we use 'What is he like?'

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: She likes dancing and singing.
questions.explanation: Dancing and singing are hobbies, so we use 'What does she like?'

questions.sentence: A: What does your dad look like? B: ___
options: He's got long brown hair and blue eyes.
questions.answer: He's got long brown hair and blue eyes.
questions.explanation: 'Look like' asks about physical appearance — we describe how someone looks.

questions.sentence: A: What is your cousin like? B: ___
questions.explanation: 'What is she like?' asks about personality — interesting and kind are personality adjectives.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: He's got dark hair and a beard.
options: What does your uncle look like?
questions.answer: What does your uncle look like?
questions.explanation: Dark hair and a beard describe physical appearance → 'What does he look like?'

questions.sentence: A: What does your sister like? B: ___
questions.explanation: 'What does she like?' asks about preferences and hobbies.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: He's very lazy and lovely.
options: What does the dog look like?
questions.explanation: 'Lazy' and 'lovely' are personality descriptions → 'What is he like?'

questions.sentence: A: What does your grandad look like? B: ___
options: He's very tall and he's got short grey hair.
questions.answer: He's very tall and he's got short grey hair.
questions.explanation: 'Look like' → appearance: tall, short grey hair.

questions.sentence: A: What is your teacher like? B: ___
questions.explanation: 'What is she like?' → personality: friendly and clever.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: They like playing basketball and eating pizza.
questions.explanation: Playing basketball and eating pizza are preferences → 'What do they like?'

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: They're unkind and unfriendly.
questions.explanation: 'Unkind' and 'unfriendly' describe personality → 'What are they like?'

questions.sentence: A: What do your parents look like? B: ___
options: My mum is short and my dad is tall.
questions.answer: My mum is short and my dad is tall.
questions.explanation: 'Look like' asks about physical appearance — height, hair, etc.

questions.sentence: A: What are your neighbours like? B: ___
questions.explanation: 'What are they like?' asks about personality — friendly and helpful.

questions.sentence: A: ___ B: She's quite tall with long black hair.
options: What does your aunt look like?
questions.answer: What does your aunt look like?
questions.explanation: Tall with long black hair = physical appearance → 'What does she look like?'

questions.sentence: My brother is very ___. He always helps people.
questions.explanation: Someone who always helps people is kind.

questions.sentence: She's got ___ hair and green eyes.
questions.explanation: 'Long blonde hair' describes physical appearance.

questions.sentence: He's very ___. He never does his homework.
questions.explanation: Someone who never does homework is lazy.

questions.sentence: My cat is very ___. It always sits on my lap.
questions.explanation: A cat that sits on your lap is lovely and affectionate.

questions.sentence: Don Quixote was very ___. He wasn't afraid of anything.
questions.explanation: Someone who isn't afraid of anything is brave.

questions.sentence: She's very ___. Everyone in school knows her and wants to be her friend.
questions.explanation: Someone everyone knows and wants to befriend is popular.

questions.sentence: He's ___ and ___. He doesn't smile and he doesn't talk to anyone.
questions.explanation: Not smiling and not talking to anyone suggests unkind and unfriendly.

questions.sentence: My teacher is very ___. She always tells us fun facts about the world.
questions.explanation: Someone who shares fun facts is interesting.

questions.sentence: She's very ___. She always gets the best marks in tests.
questions.explanation: Getting the best marks shows someone is clever.

sections.title: Zero Conditional
sections.instructions: Complete each zero conditional sentence. Remember: If/When + present simple, + present simple. Zero conditional is used for things that are always or generally true.

questions.sentence: If you ___ ice in the sun, it melts.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: If + subject + present simple. 'You' takes the base form 'put'.

questions.sentence: When water reaches 100°C, it ___.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'it' takes third person 's' → boils.

questions.sentence: If you ___ a plant, it dies.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional uses present simple in both clauses. 'You' → 'don't water'.

questions.sentence: When you ___ the mirror, you see yourself.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'look in'.

questions.sentence: If you ___ hard, you get good marks.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + present simple → 'study'.

questions.sentence: When it ___, the streets get wet.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'it' + third person 's' → 'rains'.

questions.sentence: If you ___ too much chocolate, you feel sick.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'eat'.

questions.sentence: When the sun ___, it gets dark.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'the sun' (third person) → 'goes down'.

questions.sentence: If you ___ the button, the door opens.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'press'.

questions.sentence: If you win the competition, you ___ a big prize.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + present simple → 'get'.

questions.sentence: When you heat ice, it ___ into water.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'it' + third person 's' → 'turns'.

questions.sentence: If you ___ too late, you miss the bus.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'wake up'.

questions.sentence: When I ___ a cold, I stay in bed.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'I' + base form → 'have'.

questions.sentence: If a dog ___ happy, it wags its tail.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'a dog' (third person singular) → 'is'.

questions.sentence: When you ___ water and oil, they don't mix.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'mix'.

questions.sentence: If you ___ your homework, the teacher is happy.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'do'.

questions.sentence: When the teacher ___ a question, I put my hand up.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'the teacher' (third person) → 'asks'.

questions.sentence: If William wins, he ___ to buy a fantastic new camera.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'he' + third person 's' → 'wants'.

questions.sentence: When you dance, you ___ in the mirror.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'look'. From the textbook Grammar Spotlight.

questions.sentence: If you ___ kind to people, they are kind to you.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + 'are'.

questions.sentence: When my mum ___ dinner, the kitchen smells great.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'my mum' (third person) → 'cooks'.

questions.sentence: If you ___ enough sleep, you feel tired.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional negative: 'you' → 'don't get'.

questions.sentence: When it ___ very cold, water turns into ice.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'it' + 'is'.

questions.sentence: If a baby ___ hungry, it cries.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'a baby' (third person singular) → 'is'.

questions.sentence: When you ___ metal, it expands.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'heat'.

questions.sentence: If the waiter ___ the food, the customers finish their meal.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'the waiter' (third person) → 'brings'.

questions.sentence: When you ___ a photographer, you take lots of photos.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + 'are'.

questions.sentence: If an acrobat ___ not careful, he falls.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'an acrobat' (third person singular) → 'is'.

questions.sentence: When the journalist ___ a story, she writes about it.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'the journalist' (third person) → 'finds'.

questions.sentence: If you ___ to be a singer, you practise every day.
questions.explanation: Zero conditional: 'you' + base form → 'want'.

sections.title: Jobs
sections.instructions: Match each description to the correct job from Unit 7.

pairs.prompt: This person serves food and drinks in a restaurant.
This person performs in films, TV shows or on stage.
This person prepares food in a kitchen.
This person drives a car, bus or taxi.
This person writes articles for newspapers or websites.
This person takes photos with a camera.
This person creates paintings, drawings or sculptures.
This person creates the look and style of clothes, buildings or objects.
This person sings songs, sometimes in a band or on stage.
This person performs gymnastics and tricks, often in a circus.
This person works on a farm and grows food or keeps animals.
This person works in a restaurant and people order food from them.
This person uses a camera at work and takes pictures of people or places.
This person can paint or draw very well.
This person makes clothes or creates how things look.
You have to remember a lot of words for this job and you work on a stage.
This is a popular job for people who can paint or draw very well.
This is a good job if you like food and you want to work in a kitchen.
There are lots of these at a concert for a singer and they are different colours.
A person with this job uses a car and can work in a city or for a famous pop star.
This person works for a newspaper and writes stories about events.
This person has to be very flexible and can do amazing tricks.
This person grows strawberries, beans and other food.
This person's voice is their main tool at work.
This person brings your food when you eat out.
Betty Parks works as this. She is always in the newspapers.
Su-Lin's grandparents work as these. They grow vegetables.
Jenny's dad works as this. He takes people where they need to go.
Oliver is a ___. He makes delicious meals.
Pablo's uncle is one, and also an actor.

sections.title: Personality Adjectives
sections.instructions: Match each description to the correct personality adjective from Unit 7.

pairs.prompt: Someone who is warm and always wants to help other people.
Someone who talks to everyone and makes friends easily.
Someone who learns things quickly and knows a lot.
Someone who tells you exciting stories and you never feel bored with them.
Someone who doesn't want to work and prefers to do nothing.
Someone who is sweet, cute and very nice.
Someone who is not nice and often hurts other people's feelings.
Someone who doesn't talk to people and is not welcoming.
Someone who everybody knows and likes.
Someone who is not afraid of danger or difficult things.
The opposite of 'kind'.
The opposite of 'friendly'.
The opposite of 'lazy'.
The opposite of 'boring'.
The opposite of 'cowardly' (afraid of everything).
Don Quixote is described as this because he fights giants.
Sophie is nice and warm. What adjective describes her?
Dan talks to everyone and makes friends easily. He is ___.
Harry doesn't do anything and never helps. He is ___.
Rex doesn't smile and nobody wants to be near him. He is ___.
Emma is liked by everyone and has many friends. She is ___.
A teacher who always explains things in a fun way is ___.
A student who gets the highest marks in every test is very ___.
A puppy that loves everyone and wags its tail is very ___.
A child who says mean things to others is ___.
Teachers need to be ___ and ___.
A firefighter must be very ___ to go into burning buildings.
This adjective means 'nice' and 'sweet' — perfect for babies or kittens.
If you have many friends, people might say you are ___.
Someone who never helps when you need it is ___.

sections.title: Passive Voice
sections.instructions: Choose the correct passive voice form. Remember: subject + am/is/are + past participle.

questions.sentence: English ___ in many countries around the world.
questions.explanation: Passive: English is spoken (by people) — the subject receives the action.

questions.sentence: The letters ___ by the postman every morning.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The letters (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: This book ___ by millions of children.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: This book (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: Cars ___ in factories.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: Cars (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: The museum ___ at 9 o'clock every day.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: The museum (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: Bananas ___ in tropical countries.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: Bananas (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: Football ___ all over the world.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: Football (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: The rooms ___ every day by the cleaner.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The rooms (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: Milk ___ from cows.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: Milk (uncountable/singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: The homework ___ by the teacher every Friday.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: The homework (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: These cakes ___ by my grandmother.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: These cakes (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: The school ___ at 3 o'clock every afternoon.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: The school (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: Rice ___ in China and India.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: Rice (uncountable/singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: The songs ___ by a famous singer.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The songs (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: The newspaper ___ every morning.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: The newspaper (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: Pizzas ___ in an oven.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: Pizzas (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: Spanish ___ in Spain and South America.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: Spanish (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: The windows ___ every week.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The windows (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: This photo ___ by a professional photographer.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: This photo (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: The children ___ to school by bus.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The children (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: The presents ___ in colourful paper.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The presents (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: Tea ___ with milk in England.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: Tea (uncountable/singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: The emails ___ by the secretary.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The emails (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: This castle ___ by thousands of tourists every year.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: This castle (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: Many languages ___ in Europe.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: Many languages (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: The dinner ___ by my dad on Sundays.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: The dinner (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: Diamonds ___ in South Africa.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: Diamonds (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: The dog ___ for a walk every evening.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: The dog (singular) + is + past participle.

questions.sentence: The exams ___ at the end of the year.
questions.explanation: Passive plural: The exams (plural) + are + past participle.

questions.sentence: Cheese ___ from milk.
questions.explanation: Passive singular: Cheese (uncountable/singular) + is + past participle.

sections.title: School Subjects
sections.instructions: Match each description to the correct school subject.

pairs.prompt: You work with numbers, shapes and equations in this class.
In this lesson you practise addition, subtraction and multiplication.
In this class you do experiments and learn about plants and animals.
You learn how the human body works and test ideas in this class.
You draw, paint and create pictures in this class.
In this lesson you use colours, brushes and clay to make art.
You run, jump and play team games in this class.
In this lesson you do exercises and play football or basketball outside.
You sing songs and learn to play instruments in this class.
In this lesson you listen to music and learn about rhythm and notes.
You use computers and learn about technology in this class.
In this lesson you use the Internet, apps and websites.
You learn about events from the past in this class.
In this lesson you study kings, wars and famous people from long ago.
You learn about countries, continents and maps in this class.
In this lesson you study rivers, mountains and volcanoes.
You read stories, learn grammar and practise writing in this class.
In this lesson you practise speaking and listening in a foreign language.
P.E. is the short form of this school subject.
I.T. is the short form of this school subject.

sections.title: Reading: The Altamira Caves
sections.instructions: Read the text about the Altamira Caves, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
Paula's dad is an archaeologist. Last year, he visited Spain because there is lots of work for an archaeologist in Spain. It's got many examples of how people lived in the past. His exciting new job was to study the Altamira Caves.

The caves are famous because archaeologists discovered the first cave paintings there in 1880. We did not know that in the past, people told stories using art. They didn't write like we do today.

The paintings are over 16,000 years old. Many of the paintings on the cave walls show animals and people hunting. In one cave, a really big painting showed a herd of bison. We think the paintings tell the story of how people hunted.

There were no paints or paintbrushes then, so the cave people used the things they found around them. They used sticks, bones and even their hands. They made paint out of natural materials like rocks, earth and animal fat.

Paula says: 'When I grow up, I want to be an archaeologist like my father. He tells me all about his work, and I think it is amazing!'

questions.question: What job does Paula's dad do?
Where did Paula's dad go to work?
What did the early cave people paint on the walls?
When were the cave paintings discovered?
How old are the paintings?
What did one big painting show?
What did the cave people use instead of paintbrushes?
How did cave people make paint?
options: From natural materials like rocks and earth
questions.answer: From natural materials like rocks and earth

questions.question: What does Paula want to be when she grows up?
Why is there lots of work for archaeologists in Spain?
options: It has many examples of how people lived in the past
questions.answer: It has many examples of how people lived in the past

questions.question: How did people in the past tell stories?
What does Paula think about her dad's work?

sections.title: Reading: Don Quixote
sections.instructions: Read the play script about Don Quixote, then answer the questions.
sections.passage:
A long time ago in Spain, in a land called La Mancha, there was a quiet village with a large house. The man who lived in the house was called Alonso Quixano. Alonso read books all day about brave knights. He read so much that sometimes he forgot to eat or sleep. He dreamed about saving women in danger and fighting dragons.

One day he decided to become a knight and he changed his name to Don Quixote. He put on his grandfather's armour and he rode his horse Rocinante. He asked his good friend Sancho Panza to join him and he promised to pay him lots of money in return.

Don Quixote said: 'Look, Sancho! Our next great adventure. Can you see them?'
Sancho said: 'What?'
Don Quixote said: 'Thirty or forty giants over there.'
Sancho said: 'What giants?'
Don Quixote said: 'Over there! Look how long their arms are. They're moving in all directions.'
Sancho said: 'Dear friend, you think they look like giants but they're windmills and the arms you can see are their sails blowing in the wind.'

But Don Quixote rode towards the windmills. The windmill caught Don Quixote and Rocinante and threw them down.

Sancho said: 'Are you alright? I told you that...'
Don Quixote said: 'Be quiet, Sancho! Someone changed the giants into windmills. It's magic!'
Sancho said: 'Yes, my friend. Let me help you.'

And they continued on their journey to find their next adventure.

questions.question: Where does the story take place?
What was the man's real name?
What did Alonso read about all day?
What is the name of Don Quixote's horse?
Who is Sancho Panza?
What did Don Quixote think the windmills were?
What were the 'arms' that Don Quixote saw?
What happened when Don Quixote rode towards the windmills?
What did Don Quixote say happened to the giants?
options: Someone changed them into windmills by magic
questions.answer: Someone changed them into windmills by magic

questions.question: Whose armour did Don Quixote wear?
What did Don Quixote promise Sancho?
What did Alonso sometimes forget to do because he read so much?
